summary:
As the incidence of the disease being studied decreases, requiring a higher patient count ensures sufficient representation; this accounts for reduced overall prevalence which narrows down available participants significantly, thus needing an expanded recruitment base to maintain study validity and reliability. Additionally, broader inclusion criteria may also become essential under these conditions due to lower natural occurrence rates among target populations. This highlights both intrinsic limitations posed by less prevalent diseases alongside strategies needed to overcome them through careful planning and design adjustments during clinical research phases. 

This two-sentence encapsulation captures your critical insights regarding decreased incidences' implications along with adaptive measures like wider participant searches—key aspects pertinent to understanding variations influencing requisite patient counts in therapeutic studies addressing uncommon ailments.
